coredata.dc:title: Effective synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots and their application in controllable detection of deferasirox
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yao
Pan, Gengping
Miao, Chenfang
Xu, Minge
Zhang, Menghan
Zeng, Hai
Weng, Shaohuang
Zou, Qifeng
coredata.dc:description: As an iron chelation therapy, deferasirox (DEF) is commonly used as the primary treatment drug to prevent intracellular iron overload in patients with thalassemia who require long-term and large blood transfusions. However, due to the toxic side effects of long-term use, building a convenient and sensitive DEF detection method remains an important task. This study developed a simple and effective fluorescence detection strategy for DEF based on the coordination effect of DEF and Cu2+ (DEF-Cu). The high quantum yield N-doped blue emission carbon dots (CDs) synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method were applied as the fluorescent probe. The DEF-Cu exhibited a new absorption peak centered at 334 nm, overlapping with the absorption wavelength of CDs, resulting in an internal filtration effect (IFE) for quenching the fluorescence of CDs. The quenching degrees of CDs caused by DEF-Cu showed a good linear relationship in the range of 0.5–20 μg/mL, with a detection limit (LOD) as low as 0.12 μg/mL. The accurate detections of DEF in dispersible tablets and plasma were also achieved. This strategy not only enriched the understanding of the properties of target drugs but also provided valuable insights for designing CDs with specific luminescent properties and applying them to distinctive methods for drug analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
High quantum yield
Fluorescent detection
Coordination effect
Cu2+
Deferasirox

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support from the Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province ( 2021Y9007 ), the Educational Reform Project of Fujian Medical University ( J21056 ), the National Science Foundation of Fujian Province ( 2022J01493 ), and the Research Project of Putian University ( 2023075 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM, HRTEM (top right), and size distribution (top left) of CDs. (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at 300–400 excitation. (C) The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs. (D) The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs. (E) The FTIR spectrum of CDs. The XPS spectrum (F), and high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (G), N1s (H), and O1s (I) of CDs.

Fluorescence emission spectra (A, C) and QEs (B, D) of CDs under the action of DEF, Cu2+ (A, B)/Fe3+ (C, D), and different concentrations (5, 10, 20, 40, 80 μM) of DEF with Cu2+ (A, B)/Fe3+ (C, D). The concentrations of Cu2+ and Fe3+ are both 200 μM. The inset of (A) showed fluorescence images of CDs (a), CDs-Cu (b), CDs-DEF (c), and CDs-DEF-Cu (d) under ultraviolet irradiation.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and CDs-Cu. (B) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of CDs under the action of Cu, DEF, and DEF-Cu. (C) The UV–vis absorption spectra of DEF, CDs-DEF, DEF-Cu, and CDs-DEF-Cu, and fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs. (D) The FTIR spectra of DEF and DEF-Cu. (E) The detected and corrected QE after IFE was eliminated in CDs-DEF-Cu with different DEF concentrations. The inset was the parameters used in the Formula S1, s (0.10 cm) g (0.40 cm) and d (1.00 cm) are the thickness of the excitation beam, distance and width between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette, respectively. (F) The Stern-Volmer equations and changes of F0/F of CDs-DEF-Cu at different concentrations of DEF under 298 and 320 K.

Fluorescence spectra (A) and QEs (B) of CDs-Cu at different concentrations of DEF. The insert of (B) is the linear relationship between the QEs of CDs-Cu and the concentrations of DEF. (C) the QEs of CDs-Cu to different compositions. (D) Under fixed DEF, the QEs of CDs-Cu were evaluated for 30 independent detections.
The synthesis process of high fluorescence CDs and construction a fluorescence method for deferasirox detection.

head.ce:title: Effective synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots and their application in controllable detection of deferasirox

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, The First Hospital of Putian City, Putian 351100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Hospital of Putian City

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, The First Hospital of Putian City, Putian 351100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, fluorescent carbon dots with high quantum yield were effectively prepared for the sensitive detection of deferasirox utilizing the controllable chelation of Cu2+ and deferasirox with the new absorbance peak at 334 nm through simple mixing operation.

As an iron chelation therapy, deferasirox (DEF) is commonly used as the primary treatment drug to prevent intracellular iron overload in patients with thalassemia who require long-term and large blood transfusions. However, due to the toxic side effects of long-term use, building a convenient and sensitive DEF detection method remains an important task. This study developed a simple and effective fluorescence detection strategy for DEF based on the coordination effect of DEF and Cu2+ (DEF-Cu). The high quantum yield N-doped blue emission carbon dots (CDs) synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method were applied as the fluorescent probe. The DEF-Cu exhibited a new absorption peak centered at 334 nm, overlapping with the absorption wavelength of CDs, resulting in an internal filtration effect (IFE) for quenching the fluorescence of CDs. The quenching degrees of CDs caused by DEF-Cu showed a good linear relationship in the range of 0.5–20 μg/mL, with a detection limit (LOD) as low as 0.12 μg/mL. The accurate detections of DEF in dispersible tablets and plasma were also achieved. This strategy not only enriched the understanding of the properties of target drugs but also provided valuable insights for designing CDs with specific luminescent properties and applying them to distinctive methods for drug analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Thalassemia stands as one of the most prevalent hereditary hemoglobin disorders globally. Thalassemia results in aberrant hemoglobin synthesis, diminished erythrocyte production, and associated symptoms such as elevated compensatory hematopoietic expansion and intestinal iron absorption [1]. Patients with thalassemia typically require transfusion. However, the attendant iron overload ensuing from transfusions poses a substantive risk of irreversible organ damage, notably affecting critical organs such as the heart and liver [2]. Consequently, iron chelation therapy is the primary measure to prevent intracellular iron overload [3]. Deferasirox (DEF), a conventional oral iron chelator, effectively binds excess iron and facilitates its excretion, thereby aiding in treating anemia patients grappling with iron overload resulting from prolonged transfusions [4]. Nevertheless, protracted administration of DEF may precipitate a gamut of side effects [5], encompassing hepatic dysfunction [6] and chronic kidney disease [7]. Consequently, real-time monitoring of DEF levels during administration assumes paramount importance in mitigating drug-related side effects. Presently, conventional methodologies, including chromatography strategies [8–10], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [11] and capillary electrophoresis [12] are employed for DEF quantification. Despite their demonstrable sensitivity, these techniques face challenges in widespread adoption due to the costs associated with instrumentation, intricate sample preparation protocols and the requirement for specialized operator expertise.

Recently, electrochemical methods [13], liquid–liquid microextraction [14], and optical methods [15–17] have been developed to detect DEF to overcome the challenges faced by traditional methods. Electrochemical methods leverage the electrocatalytic properties of DEF using functionalized electrodes, offering favorable linear ranges and detection limits [13,18]. While, the intricate electrode pretreatment and strict immobilization restricted the convenience of operation and possible reproducibility. Meanwhile, fluorescence techniques have gained widespread applications in the analytical sections of food, drug, and biology, ascribed to the advantages of simple and convenient operation, low cost, and high sensitivity [19,20]. Correspondingly, fluorescence methods based on fluorescent materials as probes have also been gradually employed for detecting DEF [16]. Cem Onal et al. successfully prepared a fluorescent probe for DEF detection via a pre-column derivatization process utilizing 4-bromomethyl-7-methoxycoumarin (BrMmC) as the derivatizing reagent [21]. Additionally, ligand-conjugated probes employing functional molecules have been fabricated and applied in DEF detection via fluorescence strategies [22,23]. Reported optical methods prefigure the promising prospect of fluorescence strategies for detecting DEF based on some effective probes through specific interactions. Compared with the precious probe [22] or probe prepared by extra covalent coupling [23], there is an attractive need to develop a fluorescence nanomaterial that combines affordability, ease of operation, and sensitivity as the probe, aiming for straightforward and efficient detection of DEF.

Carbon dots have garnered significant attention as versatile fluorescence carbon materials in recent years [24]. Due to their low toxicity, high stability, excellent biocompatibility, and the abundance of low-cost raw materials, carbon dots display increasing applications in biomedical science, analytical section and optoelectronic devices [25,26]. Moreover, carbon dots display tunable fluorescence properties, such as controllable absorption and emission wavelengths, modulated Stokes shift and quantum yield, which are expected to enhance the detection performance of the developed analytical methods [27–29]. Typically, carbon dots possess numerous functional groups such as carbonyl, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups. These functional groups enable interactions with analytes, thereby modulating their fluorescence intensity for detection purposes [25,30,31]. Based on the metal-assisted quenching effect, in the research on carbon dots for drug detection, the fluorescence modulation of carbon dots by metal ions allows for significant fluorescence changes when interacting with the target for the detection of DEF [23] and other drugs [32,33]. However, the responsiveness of functional groups on the surface of carbon dots to different metal ions may exhibit variations, posing a challenge on the selectivity of drug analysis when dealing with a mixture of metal ions. For the detection of DEF, there is a need to develop a kind of carbon dots exhibits little response to metal ions, but displays response based on the interaction between the target drug and metal ions. Such strategy will address the response variations of carbon dots in multi-metal environments, providing a more effective solution to the analytical complexity with enhanced performance.

Motivated by the control of preparation conditions and raw materials for synthesizing carbon dots with specific properties, this study embarked upon the synthesis of N-doped blue-emitting carbon dots (abbreviated as CDs) with elevated quantum yield (QY) via a facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis approach, employing citric acid monohydrate, L-arginine, and ethylenediamine as precursors. The fluorescence capability of CDs was enhanced due to the rich groups of amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl on the surface. Importantly, the CDs demonstrated fluorescence stability when exposed to various metal ions. Based on the chelation interaction between DEF and Cu2+ (DEF-Cu), a signal-off fluorescence sensor using CDs as a probe was constructed with a straightforward mixing protocol (Scheme 1 ). The DEF-Cu exhibited a distinct absorption peak at 334 nm, concomitantly overlapping with the absorption spectrum of CDs, thereby eliciting an inner filter effect (IFE) culminating in the quenching of CDs fluorescence. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs was directly correlated with the concentration of DEF. Under physiological conditions, through a direct mixing operation with CDs as the probe and Cu2+ as the mediator, sensitive detection of DEF was successfully achieved with excellent performance. This signal-off strategy was applied to the detection of DEF in dispersible tablets and plasma. This approach not only enriches the understanding of the properties of the target drug but also contributes insights into the design of carbon dots with tailored luminescent properties for drug analysis applications.

The 1.0 g citric acid monohydrate and 1.0 g L-arginine were mixed in a 50 mL beaker. After adding 19 mL ultrapure water and 1 mL ethylenediamine, the mixture was stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, conducted the reaction in a reaction flask at 180 °C for 6 h. The product was filtered using a 0.22 µm membrane. Then, the filtrate was purified using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 100–500 Da for 48 h. The purified solution was stored at 4 °C. And the purified solution containing the prepared CDs was applied to the characterizations of transmission electron microscopy (TEM), FTIR, and XPS according to the corresponding requirement of sample pre-treatment.

Feasibility test: A mixed solution of 20 μL of 1.5 mg/mL CDs (diluted 20 times) and 20 μL 2 mM Cu2+ was mixed with different concentrations of DEF (0, 5, 10, 20, 40, and 80 μg/mL) in a final volume of 200 μL pH 8.0 PBS and subjected to fluorescence detection after reacting at 37 °C for 1 h. Comparatively, the detection of DEF was carried out in the same procedure using Fe3+ as the coordination medium. Quenching efficiency (QE, (F0-F)/F0) was the quenching efficiency of CDs using DEF-Cu as a quencher. Among them, F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of CDs-Cu or CDs-Fe without or with the addition of DEF, respectively.

Establishing the standard curve: Mix 20 μL of 1.5 mg/mL CDs (diluted 20 times) and 20 μL of 1.5 mM Cu2+ with different concentrations of DEF solutions (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 9, 12, 16, 18, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 100 μg/mL). Dilute the mixtures with pH 8.0 PBS to a total volume of 200 μL, vortex thoroughly. Allow the reaction to proceed for 20 min at 25 °C for monitoring fluorescence.

Considering the complexity of real samples, several potential metal chelates, analogs, and common substances in blood were applied to evaluate the selectivity. The testing process of selectivity was carried out according to the same operation as DEF detection. Mix 20 μL of interferents [300 μg/mL Glucose (Glu), Arginine (Ang), Cysteine (Cyc), Glutathione (GSH), Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), D-Penicillin (D-pen), Deferoxamine (DFO), Diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA)] with 20 μL of 1.5 mg/mL CDs (diluted 20 times) and Cu2+ for the monitor of their fluorescence responses.

With approval from the Ethics Committee of the First Hospital of Putian City (2023-096), human plasma samples were obtained from informed and consenting healthy volunteers for DEF detection. Mix 20 μL of plasma (diluted 10 times) with 20 μL of 1.5 mg/mL CDs (diluted 20 times) and 20 μL of 1.5 mM Cu2+. Then, add different final concentrations (0, 2, 4, 8, 12, 16 μg/mL) of DEF standard solution. Dilute the mixtures with pH 8.0 PBS to a total volume of 200 μL, and place them in a 25 °C metal bath for 20 min, the fluorescence spectra were monitored and compared to evaluate recovery.

Moreover, the dispersible tablets (10 pieces) were dissolved in 100 mL of methanol. Filter the solution and dilute the filtrate with PBS (pH 8.0) to obtain a solution with approximately 500 mg/mL DEF. Utilize this method to determine the content of DEF in the dispersible tablet solution.

The morphology and optical properties of CDs were elucidated. TEM revealed quasi-spherical CDs with an average size of 4.24 nm (Fig. 1 A). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) revealed that the lattice spacing of CDs was 0.16 nm, indicating a graphite structure [34]. Fig. 1B illustrated the independent emission behavior of CDs under 300–400 nm excitation, attributed to the extended conjugation of the CDs' core structure [35]. The optimal excitation for achieving maximum emission at 450 nm was found to be 350 nm (Fig. 1C). Meanwhile, the UV–vis spectrum of CDs displayed two distinctive absorption peaks at 239 nm and 348 nm, attributing to π–π* and n–π* transitions, respectively [36,37]. Additionally, the QY of CDs was quantified at 63.98 %, using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference (Fig. 1D) [32].

The chemical structure and functional groups of CDs were further assessed (Fig. 1E–I). The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1E) showcased characteristic absorption peaks at 3417 cm−1 and 950 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching and bending vibrations of OH. The stretching vibrations of CO were located at 1654 cm−1. The peaks at 1552 cm−1 and 1396 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of NH and CN (amide), respectively. The peaks at 1044 cm−1 belonged to stretching vibrations of CO. Moreover, the XPS of CDs (Fig. 1F) exhibited three typical peaks at 284.35 eV, 399.81 eV, and 531.24 eV, attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The corresponding elemental contents were 63.84 % (C), 15.65 % and 20.51 % (O). The high-resolution XPS of C1s (Fig. 1G) exhibited three distinct peaks at 284.72 eV, 286.03 eV, and 287.12 eV, attributing to CC/CC, CN/CO, and CO, respectively. The N1s high-resolution XPS (Fig. 1H) illustrated two dominant peaks at 399.3 eV (pyrrolic N) and 401.5 eV (amino N) [38]. The high-resolution XPS of O1s (Fig. 1I) displayed the distinctive peaks at 530.91 eV and 531.98 eV, assigned to COH/COC and COOH, respectively [39]. The above findings indicated the successful doping of N element in CDs, accompanying with OH, NH, and COOH. The doping effect and the functional groups may be the reason for the high QY and good water dispersibility of CDs.

The optical stability of CDs was investigated. The fluorescence values of CDs were monitored for 20 consecutive days (Fig. S1A) and continuously excited at 350 nm for 1 h (Fig. S1B), demonstrating their remarkable stability over time and under prolonged excitation. Additionally, CDs exhibited temperature-independent fluorescence behavior across a range from 5 to 90 °C (Fig. S1C), as well as in solutions with varying salt concentrations from low to high (Fig. S1D). The impact of pH on the stability of CDs was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S1E, the fluorescence intensities of CDs remained relatively stable in the range of 4–11, indicating weak pH-dependent fluorescence. CDs were not resistant to strong acids (pH 3, 3.5) and alkalis (pH 12), likely due to their degradation under strong protonation and deprotonation conditions. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching efficiencies of Fe2+ and Fe3+ on CDs were 19.2 % and 23.4 % respectively, as shown in Fig. S1F, while the other metals (Na+, Ni2+, K+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Sr2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, and Ca2+) had negligible effects on the luminescence behavior of CDs. The weak response to most metal ions but relative sensitivity to Fe3+ was similar to other carbon dots used to detect iron ions [40,41].

DEF, known for its metal chelation properties, forms complexes with Cu2+ and Fe3+ through chelating action. The feasibility of using CDs as probes and Cu2+ (CDs-Cu) or Fe3+ (CDs-Fe) as a medium for DEF detection was compared. As shown in Fig. 2 A, C, the presence of DEF did not affect the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Compared with the weak effect of Cu2+ on the fluorescence intensity of CDs (Fig. 2A), CDs can be quenched by high concentration of Fe3+ to a high degree (Fig. 2C). Interestingly, simultaneous interaction of CDs with Cu2+ or Fe3+ and DEF resulted in significant fluorescence quenching of CDs, suggesting a modulation mechanism distinct from traditional “on-off-on” strategies [42–44]. In the presence of Cu2+ or Fe3+, the gradient concentrations of DEF interacted with CDs resulted in an increase in the quenching efficiencies (QEs) of CDs in the CDs-Cu (Fig. 2B). While, the QEs of CDs in the CDs-Fe exhibited irregular patterns (Fig. 2D). These results indicated the feasibility of devising a fluorescence strategy for detecting DEF using Cu2+ as a bridge and CDs as a probe.

Additionally, the luminescence efficiency of CDs and their fluorescence response to different metal ions, especially Fe3+ and Cu2+, will have a significant impact on the sensitivity of the detecting DEF. Therefore, it is necessary to optimize the synthesis process of CDs, especially the ratio of the reaction raw materials. Compared with the original CDs, a series of CDs (named CDs-1, CDs-2, and CDs-3) were obtained by adjusting the mass ratios (10:1, 4:3, 5:2) of precursor materials (citric acid and L-arginine), while keeping the reaction temperature and time (at 180 °C for 6 h) and 1 mL of ethylenediamine. Metal ion specificity assays revealed that CDs-1, CDs-2, and CDs-3, prepared at ratios of 10:1, 4:3, and 5:2, respectively, exhibited heightened sensitivity to Fe3+ and Cu2+ (Fig. S2A–C), demonstrating significantly enhanced quenching effect compared to the CDs of 1:1. Furthermore, in terms of QY, CDs-1 showed a relatively low QY of only 10.40 %. The difference in QYs among CDs-2, CDs-3, and CDs was small (Fig. S2E, F, and Fig. 1D). Considering the response to metal ions and the QY, CDs were selected as the optimal probe for detecting DEF.

The detecting mechanism of this approach was evaluated. The addition of Cu2+ did not alter the absorption peaks of CDs at 240 nm and 345 nm (Fig. 3 A), signifying that Cu2+ did not interfere with the emission behavior of CDs. The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and after the addition of Cu2+, DEF and the mixture of Cu2+ and DEF (referred to CDs-Cu, CDs-DEF, and CDs-DEF-Cu) were measured as 14.49 ns, 14.80 ns, 14.67 ns, and 14.70 ns, respectively (Fig. 3B). The nearly unchanged lifetimes suggested the absence of dynamic quenching effect (DQE) of CDs in the mixture of Cu2+ and DEF [45]. As shown in the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 3C), DEF exhibited characteristic absorption peaks at 251 nm and 294 nm, and the absorption spectrum of CDs-DEF was a simple superposition of CDs and DEF with no new absorption peaks. However, it's noteworthy that a new absorption peak at 334 nm was found in DEF-Cu, while the absorption peak at 294 nm belonging to DEF disappeared, indicating the formation of a new chelate complex between DEF and Cu2+. The interaction between Cu2+ and DEF in DEF-Cu system was examined through FTIR. The FTIR spectrum of DEF showed that the peaks at 3609 cm−1, 1687 cm−1, and 1385 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH, CO, and CN, respectively. The stretching vibrations of CC on the aromatic ring were observed at 1613 cm−1 and 1511 cm−1. Compared to DEF, DEF-Cu displayed altered intensities of absorption peaks corresponding to OH (3609 cm−1), CC (1613 cm−1 and 1511 cm−1) and CO (1687 cm−1), indicating strong interaction between Cu2+ and DEF in DEF-Cu system (Fig. 3D). Especially, the newly generated characteristic absorption peak at 334 nm of DEF-Cu overlapped with the excitation spectrum of CDs, indicating a possible inner filter effect (IFE) between DEF-Cu and CDs. Then, the QE correction was performed according to the parameters of the quartz cuvette geometry (inset in Fig. 3E) and Formula S1 [46]. As shown in Fig. 3E and Table S1, the detected and corrected QE gradually deviated with the increase in DEF concentrations. Moreover, the total fluorescence inhibition of CDs by DEF-Cu through IFE was up to 99.49 % (SE, calculated according to Formula S2). The results illustrated the main quenching mode of IFE of the quenching process of CDs by DEF-Cu [28,47]. Then, the Stern-Volmer equation of F0/F = 1 + Ksv [Q] was operated (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of probe without and with quenching agent. [Q] is the concentration of quencher. Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant). The Ksv decreased with the temperature increasing from 298 K to 320 K, showing that the newly formed non-fluorescent complex between CDs and DEF-Cu was unstable and decomposed with the increase of temperatures. The variable and decreased Ksv suggested that the presence of the static quenching effect (SQE) between CDs and DEF-Cu (Fig. 3F). The above results highlighted the dual effects of IFE and SQE of the quenching mechanism of DEF-Cu on CDs, with IFE being identified as predominant quenching mechanism.

The impact of methanol on the luminescence of CDs was investigated to determine the appropriate methanol/water ratio for the detection method. As the methanol content increased from 0 % to 80 %, the fluorescence intensities of CDs slightly increased (Fig. S3A). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensities of CDs-Cu were hardly affected by the methanol content (within 70 %). While, when the methanol content exceeded 70 %, the fluorescence signal of CDs-Cu significantly decreased (Fig. S3B). Although the DEF aqueous solution was obtained using methanol as a solubilizer, the mother solution was added to the CDs-Cu system through different dilution ratios, and the final methanol content was less than 10 %. Therefore, the impact of methanol on the detection system was negligible.

Other factors were investigated. The fluorescence values of CDs, CDs-Cu, and CDs-DEF-Cu remained almost consistent at different reacting periods (5–180 min, Fig. S4A) and temperatures (4–60 °C, Fig. S4B). For the convenience of detection, the reaction time and temperature were set at 20 min and 25 °C, respectively. Simultaneously, within the pH range of 5.0–11.0, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the CDs-DEF-Cu system remains relatively stable. However, outside of this range, there were significant fluctuations in fluorescence QEs (Fig. S4C and Fig. S4D). This was likely due to the presence of copper ions coordinating with DEF in the system, and the differences in the hydrolysis of copper ions at different pH conditions resulted in variations in the equilibrium, leading to diverse forms of copper existence. Thus, considering the hydrolysis of Cu2+ in alkaline conditions and the unsuitability of strong alkaline environments for biological samples, pH 8.0 was selected as the optimal condition. In addition, the optimization experiments on the concentration of Cu2+ were also conducted (Fig. S4E). As the concentration of Cu2+ increased, the QEs of the CDs-DEF-Cu system gradually rises and stabilizes at 150 μM. Therefore, we selected this concentration as the final copper ion concentration for the system (Fig. S4E).

Under the optimal conditions, the detection of DEF using the CDs-Cu system was established. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, with increasing concentrations of DEF, the fluorescence signals of the CDs-Cu system gradually decreased. The QEs of DEF-Cu towards CDs increased progressively and eventually reached a plateau (Fig. 4B). This phenomenon can be attributed to the saturation of DEF-Cu formation due to the limited availability of Cu2+ ions. The quenching degrees of CDs caused by DEF-Cu showed a good linear relationship in the range of 0.5–20 μg/mL (Fig. 4B), with a linear equation of Y = 0.09454 + 0.02626CDEF, R2 = 0.9914. And the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.12 μg/mL based on a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3. Comparative analysis with other methods for DEF (Table S2) underscored the excellent performance of this fluorescence strategy based on the CDs-Cu system in terms of linear range and LOD. Moreover, this straightforward operation procedure was easy-control through simple mixing process. The selectivity of this method was investigated. The common interferents, such as Glu, Ang, Cyc, GSH, EDTA, D-Pen, DFO, DTPA, etc., were tested. As depicted in Fig. 4C, while DEF showed a significant response, the interfering substances exhibited minimal response in this testing system, demonstrating the method's high selectivity towards DEF. Furthermore, the repeatability of the method was rigorously investigated by measuring 30 samples of 12 μg/mL DEF (Fig. 4D). The results demonstrated close agreement among replicates, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.62 %, affirming the high reproducibility and reliability of this method.

The accuracy of the method for determining DEF in real samples was further determined. After adding different concentrations of DEF (2, 4, 8, 12, 16 μg/mL) to human plasma samples, the spiked recoveries ranged from 87.02 % to 112.54 %, with RSDs of 0.37 % to 4.18 % (Table S3). Following the elimination of other interfering factors (such as using iron ion masking agents to eliminate interference from Fe2+ in plasma), this method is expected to be applicable for monitoring DEF in clinic. Using dispersed DEF dispersible tablets as the actual sample, the reliability and practical application of the method in pharmaceutical quality control were also evaluated. Based on the detected result of DEF content, the percentage content of the label amount was calculated to be 94.31 % with an RSD of 2.24 % (n = 3) (Table S4), which met the requirement of the Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2020). The results indicated that this method had the potential to be applied to the detection of DEF in drug quality control.

In this study, with the assistance of ethylenediamine, N-doped blue emission carbon dots with high quantum yield and weak response to metal ions were successfully synthesized and screened by one-step hydrothermal method using the mass ratio of 1:1 of citric acid monohydrate and L-arginine. Inspired by the chelating interaction between DEF and metal ions, as well as the optical properties of CDs, a simple and effective fluorescence detection strategy was developed for DEF by combining the reaction system of DEF and Cu2+ with CDs as the probe. Additionally, based on the formation of a new complex (DEF-Cu) between DEF and Cu2+, a new absorption peak was observed at 334 nm, which coincided with the absorption peak of CDs, leading to an IFE and quenching of the fluorescence of CDs. The QE of CDs was directly related to the concentration of DEF with excellent testing performance. Combined with the real applications of this method in plasma and dispersed tablets, the strategy based on specially designed CDs and the interaction of metal ions will provide valuable insights for designing CDs as probes to analyze clinically known drugs with understood properties.

Yao Wang: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing–original draft, Writing–review & editing. Gengping Pan: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis. Chenfang Miao: Data curation, Writing–review & editing. Minge Xu: Data curation, Writing–review & editing. Menghan Zhang: Formal analysis. Hai Zeng: Resources. Shaohuang Weng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing–original draft, Writing–review & editing. Qifeng Zou: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration, Writing–review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (2021Y9007), the Educational Reform Project of Fujian Medical University (J21056), the National Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2022J01493), and the Research Project of Putian University (2023075).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111536.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thalassaemia
Lancet

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Taher AT, Weatherall DJ, Cappellini MD. Thalassaemia. Lancet, 2018, 391 (10116): 155-167.
